content.full_text:
CITY OF LOMPOC
VALLEY OF FLOWERS OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY

December 9, 1991

General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 South J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Request for Formal Advice

Dear General Counsel:

I have been authorized by Lompoc City Mayor J.D. Smith and
Councilmember Karl G. Braun to request your formal written advice
in accordance with Government Code Section 83114 regarding the
following conflict of interest question.

BACKGROUND AND OVERVIEW

On September 3, 1991, staff was directed by a councilmember to
propose an amendment to Lompoc City Code Section 16-23 (a copy of
which is attached) in order to remedy the regressive rate structure
of the business tax for rental units. This direction to staff was
made during the hearing on the appeal by Mr. George Johnson
concerning the amount of his tax liability under Section 16-23.
Receiving an adverse ruling from the City Council, Mr. Johnson has
subsequently filed a lawsuit challenging that decision in Superior
Court. In addition, Mr. Johnson has filed a complaint with the
Fair Political Practices Commission's Enforcement Division against
Mayor J.D. Smith and Councilmember Karl G. Braun (FPPC No. 91-414).
The gist of the complaint was that Mayor Smith and Councilmember
Braun each owned rental units and therefore were subject to the tax
due under Section 16-23, but failed to disqualify themselves from
voting on Mr. Johnson's appeal. The Commission determined that the
allegations were unfounded and closed the file without formal
enforcement action.

The present request differs from FPPC No. 91-414 in that the
proposed City Council action involves a vote on an amendment to
Lompoc City Code Section 16-23 with respect to altering the
regressive rate structure of the rental unit business tax to
provide for a more equitable rate structure. Whereas the
underlying facts in FPPC No. 91-414 involved the applicability of
Section 16-23 to one individual taxpayer, the proposed City Council
action which is the subject of this request has the potential to
affect the rental unit business tax liability for every owner of
three or more rental units within the City of Lompoc, including
Mayor Smith and Councilman Braun.

CITY HALL, 100 CIVIC CENTER PLAZA, P.O. BOX 8001, LOMPOC, CA 93438-8001
(805) 736-1261; FAX: (805) 736-5347

General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
December 9, 1991
Page 2


The concern of Mayor Smith and Councilman Braun is whether they may
vote on or participate in the consideration of amending Section 16-
23, or whether they have a conflict of interest requiring them to
abstain from voting or participating. As Lompoc is a general law
city, the disqualification of Mayor Smith and Councilmember Braun
would not prevent a quorum from voting on the amendment of Section
16-23.

Property Owned by Mayor Smith

As revealed by the attached Form 721, Mayor Smith owns one rental
property in which his financial interest exceeds $10,000 located
at 237 North D Street in Lompoc, consisting of three units. Under
Lompoc City Code Section 16-23, rental properties of less than
three units are exempt from any tax. Mayor Smith's tax liability
under Section 16-23 is $25.00 per year.

Property Owned by Councilmember Braun

As revealed by the attached Form 721, Councilman Braun owns rental
properties in which his financial interest exceeds $10,000 located
at 230 North C Street and 324-328-332 North L Street in Lompoc,
consisting of a total of 12-14 units. Councilmember Braun's tax
liability under Section 16-23 is $50.00 per year.

ANALYSIS

In order to set forth our understanding of the applicable statutes
and regulations, we have taken the liberty of providing the
following analysis, which we hope is of assistance to your office.
Government Code Section 87100 prohibits public officials from
making, participating in making, or attempting to use their
official position to influence, a governmental decision in which
they have a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an
official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official or on any real property which the official has an
interest of $1,000 or more. Regulation § 18703 provides that a
material financial effect of a governmental decision on an
official's interests is distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally unless the decision will affect the official's
interest in substantially the same manner as it will affect all
members of the public or a significant segment of the public.

General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
December 9, 1991
Page 4




Based on Ferraro and Letter I-91-113, we believe that your office
should determine that inasmuch as Mayor Smith owns no more than
three rental units within the City, Mayor Smith is not disqualified
from voting on or participating in the consideration of the
proposed amendment to Section 16-23 under the public generally
exception.

The remaining question is whether Councilmember Braun is
disqualified. As noted above, under Ferraro the public generally
exception is inapplicable to owners of four or more units.
Inasmuch as Councilmember Braun owns 12-14 units, it would appear
that the public generally exception cannot apply to his situation.
In footnote 3 in Ferraro, however, the Commission recognized the
public generally exception may apply if landlords are a predominant
"trade, industry or profession". The Commission proceeded to
determine that in Los Angeles, landlords were not a predominant
trade, industry or profession. While the percentage of rental
units in the City of Los Angeles was not apparent from the Ferraro
decision, it is submitted that the percentage of rental units in
the City of Lompoc may indeed by higher than in the City of Los
Angeles. According to the City of Lompoc's Planning Department,
who base their information on the 1990 census, 48.14 percent of the
City's total housing stock is occupied by renters (6,020 units),
while only 51.86 percent of the City's total housing stock is
owner-occupied (6,484 units). Based on these statistics, we
encourage your office to make an independent determination of
whether landlords are a predominant trade, industry or profession
in the City of Lompoc.

Assuming, however, that the trade, industry or profession doctrine
does not apply, Councilmember Braun may avoid disqualification only
if it can be determined that his participation will not have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his interest.
Under Regulation § 18702.1(a)(3)(C), a decision has a material
effect if it involves the imposition, repeal or modification of any
taxes or fees assessed or imposed on real property in which the
official has a direct or indirect interest of $1,000 or more. If
this rule is applicable without admitting of any exceptions, it
might appear that Councilmember Braun's participation would
necessarily have a material financial effect on his interest. In
Letter I-91-113, however, the Commission applied a different
materiality standard, Regulation § 18702.2(g). Under that
standard, Councilmember Braun's decision would not have a material
financial effect on his interest as the decision would not affect
his gross revenues or the value of his assets or liabilities by
$10,000 or more and would not result in his incurring of additional

General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
December 9, 1991
Page 3


In John Ferraro, Councilmember, Los Angeles 4 FPPC 62 (No. 78-009,
Nov. 7, 1978), the question was whether a councilmember who owns
a single-family rental property may vote on or participate in the
consideration of a proposed rent control ordinance. The Commission
concluded that the interests of owners of three or fewer rental
units will not be affected by rent control decisions in a manner
distinguishable from the effect upon a significant segment of the
public generally, and therefore such owners are not disqualified.
The Commission reasoned that owners of three or fewer rental units
could not be considered part of the rental property industry. As
the proposed rent control ordinance would have affected all owners
of three or fewer rental units in much the same manner, the public
generally exception applied. The Commission also stated:

     Our decision in this matter does not mean that an owner
     of four or more rental units will automatically be
     disqualified from participating in rent control
     decisions.    Although the owner of four or more units
     cannot avoid disqualification through use of the public
     generally provision of Section 87103, he may still
     participate in rent control decisions if the
     circumstances indicate that it is not foreseeable that
     the decisions will have a material effect on his
     financial interests.   While we believe that in most
     instances it will be foreseeable that rent control
     decisions will have a material effect upon the financial
     interest of the owners of four or more rental units, it
     is not impossible to imagine situations where a proposed
     rent control ordinance would not materially affect those
     interests.

Id. at 7.   Because the public generally exception governed the
outcome of the decision, the Commission did not find it necessary
to address questions of foreseeability and materiality.

In an informal assistance letter (I-91-113, June 17, 1991) issued
by your office, the question was whether a city councilmember who
owns one rental unit may participate in a governmental decision
involving a business license fee exemption for owners of a small
number of rental units in a jurisdiction where 50 percent of the
residential properties include at least one rental unit.   The
Commission concluded that the "public generally" exception applied
to officials who own one rental unit because the effect of the
decision on their rental property rental business interest would be
similar to the effect of that decision on a significant segment of
the public.

General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
December 9, 1991
Page 5


expenses or a reduction or elimination of existing expenses of
$2,500 or more.  Because of the uncertainty as to which standard
is applicable, clarification as to the appropriate standard for
determining materiality is requested.

CONCLUSION

Based on the foregoing analysis of the applicable statutes,
regulations, and Commission opinions, it appears that Mayor Smith
may vote on and participate in the consideration of amending Lompoc
City Code Section 16-23 because he owns no more than three rental
units within the City.  Councilmember Braun also will be able to
vote and participate if the materiality standards of Regulation §
18702.2(g) are applicable as suggested by Letter I-91-113, but may
be disqualified if the standards of Regulation § 18702.1(a)(3)(C)
are applicable instead.  We recognize that our conclusions are
tentative in nature and therefore request formal written advice.

I trust that the foregoing analysis will be of some assistance.
If additional specificity is required from this office, please do
not hesitate to contact me directly at any time.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

John Eastman
Deputy City Attorney

c:  Mayor J.D. Smith
    Councilmember Karl Braun
    David Hirsch, City Attorney
    Maureen Bosking, City Clerk

Attachments

sections.question: The concern of Mayor Smith and Councilman Braun is whether they may vote on or participate in the consideration of amending section 16-23, or whether they have a conflict of interest requiring them to abstain from voting or participating.
sections.question_synthetic: May Mayor Smith and Councilman Braun, who own rental properties subject to Lompoc City Code Section 16-23's business tax, vote on and participate in an amendment to that tax code that would alter the rate structure and potentially affect all rental property owners in the city?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from City Attorney to FPPC requesting formal advice. The letter includes extensive analysis of applicable law and prior FPPC decisions (Ferraro, Letter I-91-113) but this is the city's own analysis, not FPPC's response. No formal FPPC conclusion section present. Document quality is poor due to OCR scanning artifacts but content is substantially readable.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The concern of Mayor Smith and Councilman Braun is whether they may vote on or participate in the consideration of amending section 16-23, or whether they have a conflict of interest requiring them to abstain from voting or participating.

embedding.summary: The City of Lompoc requests formal FPPC advice on whether Mayor Smith (3 rental units) and Councilman Braun (12-14 rental units) have a disqualifying conflict of interest that would prevent them from voting on a proposed amendment to the city's business tax code for rental units.
